text: Hello and welcome to the Imagine AI Live podcast. I'm your host for this event, Sharanik. We are here in New York City for the first Impact event, and I have a very special guest with me now. I have Daniel. Welcome. Hello, Sharanik. Thanks for having me again. It's good to see you again. Yes, it's been a while since Vegas. It's been a while. Well, welcome on. Please introduce yourself and tell the audience a little bit about what you're working on. Excellent. Yeah. So my name is Daniel Karendi-Mani. I'm a founder and CEO of a company called Xperia. We are in the process of building an AI power knowledge management platform designed specifically for professional services and consulting firms. Awesome. So explain to me what your ideal client would be like and how they would use your product. So any consulting firm, and my background is in consulting, so obviously I understand the industry well. And when you work in consulting, one thing you're challenged with is this concept of retaining and recalling your knowledge, right? So if someone comes in your organization, they may not know anything you know. And if someone leaves, you may lose a lot of their institutional knowledge. And, you know, those consulting organizations suffer from this. So what we add about is providing them with a capability to enhance what they do. So this can address any midsize consulting firm, any consulting firm that's in the business of providing value to their client by reusing what they've done in the past. Okay, nice. So how do you get that information and how do you organize it or make it easily accessible? What is the process like? Yeah, that's a great question. So when you think about consulting firm and professional services, they are working in usually between 10 and 15 applications every day. So there's content scattered across a lot of different systems, right? And that's a challenge, right, because you're constantly trying to think, where can I find that information? In most cases, you don't find it. So our concept is to go out and connect to all of those different systems that have digital source, like it could be a Google Drive, a Box Drive, or Microsoft SharePoint Drive. It could be Slack messages. It could be meetings recording. It could be your time and billing system or your CRM system and there a lot of information great knowledge in those systems So we going to take it all curate it using of course AI and some special algorithm and proprietary things we're building to take that knowledge out, curate it, and create a virtual knowledge map of what you have, right, so that it can be reused and recalled using, of course, generative AI and chatbots of the like. Yeah. So with anything that's using information or knowledge like you're doing, there are, of course, security concerns. How have you addressed any of those issues? Great question as well. And that's interesting because obviously the larger the firm, the less likely you're going to have access to everything. So at the same time, we need to be concerned about privacy and that you only can see what you have access to. And by the way, there's been a great problem of Gen AI in general today is people are discovering that all of a sudden they have access to things they shouldn't have access to. So we're taking a privacy and security first approach. As we're extracting the content, we're extracting not only what the content is and its knowledge, but who has access to it. So that when you ask a question and you try to retrieve it, it's being filtered down to only the people that have access to it. Nice. Very good. And how did you end up getting connected with the Imagine AI Live community? So, I mean, obviously, I went to the last show in Vegas in March, and we met there. You know, I found about the conference back probably in December of 2023. I was looking for places to go network about AI, and I felt like this was a very interesting conference. It's not sponsored by big tech or, you know, vendors like most conferences are. It was agnostic, but more importantly, it was targeted business users and leaders, right, which really attracted me because I am essentially a technologist but also a business person, and I thought it was a great place to go network with like-minded people. Awesome. So we're here at the Cornell Tech campus, which is a very special place to be, but it also brings up the question of education today. So for those that are entering into university and seeking out an education, is there any advice you'd give to them surrounding AI and technology integrated into that? Absolutely. I think that every student needs to understand that AI is now their companion or their mini coach to help them go through learning learning faster and better right And they have to be conscious of using AI in a way that beneficial to their learning activities And not a hindrance, right? And not a hindrance, right? So when you ask a question to a chat GPT or whatever chatbot you're using, don't say, tell me the answer. Instead, say, tell me how to get to the answer. and the step-by-step approach to understand the problem. And if you're struggling on something, the same thing. Don't ask, you know, what's the answer, but ask, how do I go back and what other step I should take to get a better answer? Yeah, help me brainstorm how to. Help me brainstorm, absolutely. Yeah. Yeah, AI is definitely changing education. Absolutely. But, you know, we've seen, you know, I think institutions, universities and colleges and schools, high school and the like, have to think back about how they can embrace it and not prevent it. I got to speak with the dean here at the Cornell Tech campus this morning, and he has a very good attitude about AI and technology and integrating it into education. He's very open-minded about it, and that's a nice attitude to see that is going to be educating the next generation of people. Yeah, we have to. Yeah, it's great. So what about those that are already in the workforce or about to enter the workforce, what advice would you give to them? The same advice is think about what you're doing every day and imagine if you had a companion or co-pilot that can help you achieve your task faster, better, with higher quality, where you can now focus your brain power and your human energy to something that's more valuable than mundane routine, doing the same thing over and over. Right. And you have to be open minded. I've seen many, many folks that say, yeah, I've tried it. Didn't work. I'm done. Right. And I always insist, I say, you have to get to a point where you don't try it once. You keep trying. You keep trying. Educate yourself. There's amazing resource available from podcast videos, everything online where you can educate yourself so that you become a powerful person, a person with new capabilities you didn't have before. Absolutely. So on the topic of AI do you have any fears or what are the biggest challenges in the progression of AI I think you know when you think about new technology in the past they been adopted slowly right And it's been going step by step. And I think technology was always intended to achieve one goal, right? So if you had a software, it was to do something. If it was a CRM software, it'd help you just be better at managing your customer. What's interesting with AI, specifically large language model, is that they have unintended use, unexpected use. People are using it for ways that it wasn't intended, but in good ways, right? You know, for instance, you know, you can use a large language model to do things that the makers and open AI didn't expect people to use, right? So you're seeing more and more of this, which makes it unpredictable and brings some ethical and safety concerns, right, of, you know, are you using it the right way? Are you paying attention to the risk, right, because, you know, AI is not perfect. And if you just believe what's being said by an AI agent, then you may be getting the wrong information, right? So there's a lot of challenge. And I think that, you know, I've spoken about that before, is there's still a lack of regulation and a lack of guardrails. And I think commercial organizations are probably not the best one to just put their own guardrails. They're trying. We're going to have to see how that happens. But, you know, at least in Europe, there's some regulation coming. Here in the U.S. as well, we're hoping for more, obviously. Yeah, absolutely. So on the flip side of that, what are your hopes and what do you think AI can do to benefit people in the near future? I mean, amazing things. When you think about the power of AI, I do strongly believe that AI will help solve climate change, right? And will help solve cancer and diseases that couldn't be solved before. Will give humans the ability to do things that you just couldn't do before, right? It will allow people that are not as fortunate to be able to learn things that they couldn't otherwise. So I think it has an amplifying power that's amazing and exciting. But it also brings a lot of fear for people. It goes so fast that it scares a lot of people. But it's here. It's here.
